coredata.dc:title: Effect of sludge humic acid-derived nano-biochars on anaerobic degradation of sulfamethoxazole by Shewanella oneidensis MR-1
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Peiwen
Li, Qiansheng
Lu, Hong
Fu, Ze
Zhou, Jiti
Sun, Chenghao
Wang, Xuehai
coredata.dc:description: Some nano-biochars (nano-BCs) as electron mediators could enter into cells to directly promote intracellular electron transfer and cell activities. However, little information was available on the effect of nano-BCs on SMX degradation. In this study, nano-BCs were prepared using sludge-derived humic acid (SHA) and their effects on SMX degradation by Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 were investigated. Results showed that nano-BCs (Carbon dots, CDs, <10 nm) synthesized using SHA performed a better accelerating effect than that of the nano-BCs with a larger size (10–100 nm), which could be attributed to the better electron transfer abilities of CDs. The degradation rate of 10 mg/L SMX in the presence of 100 mg/L CDs was significantly increased by 84.6% compared to that without CDs. Further analysis showed that CDs could not only be combined with extracellular Fe(III) to accelerate its reduction, but also participate in the reduction of 4-aminobenzenesulphonic acid as an intermediate metabolite of SMX via coupling with extracellular Fe(III) reduction. Meanwhile, CDs could enter cells to directly participate in intracellular electron transfer, resulting in 32.2% and 25.2% increases of electron transfer system activity and ATP level, respectively. Moreover, the activities of SMX-degrading enzymes located in periplasm and cytoplasm were increased by around 2.2-fold in the presence of CDs. These results provide an insight into the accelerating effect of nano-BCs with the size of <10 nm on SMX degradation and an approach for SHA utilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sludge-derived carbon dots
Sulfamethoxazole
Degradation
Electron transfer
Shewanella oneidensis MR-1

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size effects of nano-BCs on SMX degradation
Role of CDs during SMX degradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open Foundation of National Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Petrochemical Environmental Pollution Prevention and Control Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Open Foundation of National Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Petrochemical Environmental Pollution Prevention and Control Technology (Grant No. 34880000-22-ZC0607-0117 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The electron transfer capacities of SHA and nano-BCs; (b) their effects on SMX degradation; (c) ETSA of strain MR-1. (b,c) Reaction conditions: 15 mg/L SMX, 2 mM Fe(III) (*p ≤ 0.05).
Morphology of CDs. (a) TEM image with the inset of the size distribution; (b) HRTEM image; (c) XRD pattern.
Surface groups and elemental composition of CDs. (a) FTIR spectrum; (b) XPS of C 1s; (c) XPS of O 1s; (d) XPS of N 1s.
Effect of CDs concentration on 10 mg/L SMX degradation (a) and LDH release assays of strain MR-1 at 36 h (b).
Effects of Fe(III), CDs (a) and SMX concentration (b) on SMX degradation by strain MR-1. Reaction conditions: 100 mg/L CDs, 2 mM Fe(III).
Effect of CDs on Fe(III) reduction during SMX degradation. Reaction conditions: 100 mg/L CDs, 2 mM Fe(III), 10 mg/L SMX.
Distribution of CDs. (a) Extracellular FL intensity at different times; (b) FL intensity of cell extracts.

Effect of 100 mg/L CDs on the activity and electron transfer ability of cells. (a) ATP; (b) NADH/NAD+ and NADH; (c) complex I and complex III. Statistically significant differences were considered when p-values were less than 0.05 (*: p < 0.05, and ***: p ≤ 0.001).

head.ce:title: Effect of sludge humic acid-derived nano-biochars on anaerobic degradation of sulfamethoxazole by Shewanella oneidensis MR-1

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SINOPEC (Dalian) Research Institute of Petroleum and Petrochemicals Co. Ltd, Dalian, 116045, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SINOPEC (Dalian) Research Institute of Petroleum and Petrochemicals Co. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: SINOPEC (Dalian) Research Institute of Petroleum and Petrochemicals Co. Ltd, Dalian, 116045, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Some nano-biochars (nano-BCs) as electron mediators could enter into cells to directly promote intracellular electron transfer and cell activities. However, little information was available on the effect of nano-BCs on SMX degradation. In this study, nano-BCs were prepared using sludge-derived humic acid (SHA) and their effects on SMX degradation by Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 were investigated. Results showed that nano-BCs (Carbon dots, CDs, <10 nm) synthesized using SHA performed a better accelerating effect than that of the nano-BCs with a larger size (10–100 nm), which could be attributed to the better electron transfer abilities of CDs. The degradation rate of 10 mg/L SMX in the presence of 100 mg/L CDs was significantly increased by 84.6% compared to that without CDs. Further analysis showed that CDs could not only be combined with extracellular Fe(III) to accelerate its reduction, but also participate in the reduction of 4-aminobenzenesulphonic acid as an intermediate metabolite of SMX via coupling with extracellular Fe(III) reduction. Meanwhile, CDs could enter cells to directly participate in intracellular electron transfer, resulting in 32.2% and 25.2% increases of electron transfer system activity and ATP level, respectively. Moreover, the activities of SMX-degrading enzymes located in periplasm and cytoplasm were increased by around 2.2-fold in the presence of CDs. These results provide an insight into the accelerating effect of nano-BCs with the size of <10 nm on SMX degradation and an approach for SHA utilization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The production and wide use of sulfanilamide antibiotics lead to their release and residues in various environments (Prasannamedha and Kumar, 2020). As one of the most persistent sulfonamides, sulfamethoxazole has been frequently detected in various water systems including wastewaters, surface waters, and groundwaters (Zhang et al., 2010). Moreover, SMX in aquatic environments can accelerate the spread of antibiotic resistance genes in microbial populations, induce genetic mutations and even disrupt ecosystem (Ding et al., 2021; Reguyal and Sarmah, 2018; Wang et al., 2023). Thus, SMX treatment has received much attention. Bio-treatment method has been used to degrade SMX due to low cost and less secondary pollution (Wang and Wang, 2018). However, as microbial metabolism rate is low, anaerobic biodegradation of SMX is a slow process.

A recent study showed that humic analog AQDS could be used as an electron mediator to enhance SMX removal based on the redox property of its quinone group via increasing extracellular Fe(III) reduction rate and cell activity (Zhou et al., 2018). Thus, it is assumed that humic acid has a similar effect on SMX removal (Kemmou et al., 2018). Except for quinone groups, humic acid generally contains other oxygen-containing functional groups such as phenolic hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Wang et al., 2021a, 2021b). This structure is beneficial for preparing biochar containing redox groups. Biochar is generally derived from the pyrolysis of agricultural and forestry waste and other biomass waste under anoxic conditions. As biochar, an electron mediator, is of good conductivity, they are superior to humic acid. Biochar could be physically or biologically degraded to nano-sized particles during its application. When the size of nano-biochars (nano-BCs) is less than 10 nm, they (named carbon dots, CDs) could enhance intracellular electron transfer via entering into cells (Liu et al., 2020). However, little information is available on the effect of nano-BCs as electron mediators on SMX removal.

It was reported that humic acid had been extensively utilized to prepare nano-BCs for chemical analysis, photovoltaic conversion, bio-imaging, and drug delivery (He et al., 2022). Considering that sludge-derived humic acid (SHA) resists to be biodegraded, and are important inhibitory compound during the hydrolysis and methanogenesis processes of waste sludge (Azman et al., 2016; Li et al., 2019a, 2019b), SHA was used to prepare nano-BCs with the sizes of 10–100 nm and <10 nm in this study. Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 capable of degrading SMX (Zhao et al., 2022) and reducing Fe(III) was used as a model strain. Fe(III) was used as an electron acceptor, the size effects and electron transfer performances of nano-BCs on SMX degradation by strain MR-1 were investigated. Especially, the accelerating effects of extracellular and intracellular CDs on SMX degradation were studied in detail, which is beneficial for further understanding the role of SHA-derived CDs as electron mediators during SMX degradation.

SMX (99%), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) (99%), diquat dibromide monohydrate (DQ) (99%) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin (China). Methanol, acetic acid and acetonitrile were of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade and obtained from Oceanpak (Sweden). The other reagents were analytical grade.

S. oneidensis MR-1 was cultured in Luria Bertani (LB) medium containing 5.0 g/L yeast extract, 10.0 g/L peptone and 10.0 g/L NaCl (pH = 7.2). Anaerobic medium was composed of (g/L): 1.5 K2HPO4, 17.1 NaH2PO4·12H2O, 1.0 NH4Cl, 2.0 yeast extract, 2.0 lactate, 0.1 MgCl2, 0.02 CaCl2 (pH = 7.2).

The excess sludge was obtained from Lingshuihe wastewater treatment plant (Dalian, China). The extraction process of humic acid was referred to the reported work with some modifications (Liu et al., 2019). Briefly, the above excess sludge was first adjusted to pH = 3 with 6 M HCl and stirred for 1 h. The supernatant containing heavy metals was removed by using centrifugation at 10,000×g for 10 min. The obtained precipitate was dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH, aerated with nitrogen, shaken at 150 rpm for 4 h and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then adjusted to pH = 1–2 with 6 M HCl and remained for 12 h. The precipitate was obtained and dissolved in the solution containing 0.1 M KOH, in which the concentration of K+ reached 0.3 M by adding KCl. The solution was then shaken at 150 rpm for 4 h and then centrifuged to collect supernatant. The supernatant was adjusted to pH = 1 with 6 M HCl and remained for 12 h. Then the obtained precipitate by using centrifugation was added into the solution containing 0.1 M HCl and 0.3 M HF (1:1 v/v) and remained for 12 h. The solid used as SHA was obtained by using centrifugation and freeze-drying.

1 g of the SHA was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water by using sonication for 10 min. The solution was placed in a 100 mL hydrothermal autoclave liner in an oven at 200 °C and reacted for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered using a filter membrane (0.1 μm) to obtain the nano-BCs with particle size <100 nm. These nano-BCs were further dialyzed with 1 kD and 100 kD dialysis bags (Fig. S1) to collect biochar nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 10 to 100 nm in the 100 kD dialysis bag, and CDs with sizes of <10 nm in the space between the dialysis bags of 1 kD and 100 kD. The used CDs were collected and purified with 1 kD dialysis bags for 24 h, and were then further freeze-dried for the following characterization.

Strain MR-1 was aerobically cultured in LB medium. After 36 h of the incubation, cells were collected by centrifugation at 10,000×g for 5 min, washed three times and resuspended in 10 mM phosphate buffer for the following experiments. Batch degradation experiments were carried out in 60 mL serum bottles containing 50 mL anaerobic medium supplemented with certain concentrations of SMX and FeCl3. Then these bottles were purged with N2 for 20 min, and tightly sealed with rubber stoppers and aluminum caps. All of these bottles were autoclaved at 121 °C for 20 min. The prepared nano-BCs materials and the above cell suspensions (OD600 = 0.2) were added into these bottles, followed by incubation with 150 rpm at 35 °C.

Effects of SMX (10–30 mg/L), Fe(III) (1–6 mM), and nano-BCs materials with sizes of 10–100 nm and <10 nm (0–200 mg/L) were studied. The effects of 2 mM Fe(III) and 100 mg/L CDs on the degradation of 10 mg/L 4-aminobenzenesulphonic acid (4-ABSA) were tested. The controls without cells, Fe(III) and CDs were also carried out. Samples in reaction systems were taken every 12 h for detecting SMX concentration. Under the optimal conditions, the concentrations of Fe(III) and Fe(II), ATP, electron transport system activity (ETSA), NADH reductase (complex I) and bc1 cytochrome (complex III) of cells in the reaction systems were measured. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

The cells were incubated in 50 mL anaerobic medium containing 100 mg/L CDs and 2 mM Fe(III) at 35 °C and 150 rpm. After 72 h, the cells were harvested by centrifugation at 10,000×g for 10 min. All subsequent steps were carried out at 4 °C unless otherwise stated. The obtained cells were suspended in a cold phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.5) containing 10% glycerol, 2 mM EDTA and 1 mM dithiothreitol. The cell suspension was sonicated for 30 min in an ice bath with a Sonifier (225 W). Unbroken cells were removed by centrifugation at 10,000×g for 5 min. The crude extracts were then subjected to ultracentrifugation at 150,000×g for 2 h. The obtained precipitation was re-suspended in the above-mentioned phosphate buffer and used as membrane proteins. The supernatant was used as a mixture of cytoplasmic and periplasmic proteins. The activities of the SMX-degrading enzymes were tested using cell extracts. The reaction mixture (1 mL) consisted of 0.3 mg/mL crude enzyme, 10 mg/L CDs, 0.1 mM NADH, and 1 mg/L SMX in a phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.5). The control experiment was also conducted without CDs. The reaction mixture was incubated in an anaerobic incubator at 35 °C for 30 min. A unit (U) of SMX-degrading enzyme activity was defined as the amount of enzyme required to degrade 1 nmol per min of SMX at 35 °C (Zhang et al., 2014). The content of total proteins was measured by using the Bradford method (Lucarini and Kilikian, 1999). The fluorescence (FL) intensities of the extracted cytoplasmic, periplasmic and membrane proteins with/without CDs incubation were then measured to determine the distribution of CDs (λex = 365 nm,λem = 448 nm).

Morphological features of CDs were tested by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan) at 300 kV. The crystal structure of CDs was collected on the X-ray diffraction diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). The composition and functional groups of CDs were detected by using Fourier transform spectrometer (Nicolet iS20, Thermo Scientific, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. Their FL properties were tested using a temperature-controlled fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse 03030429, Agilent, USA). The electron-donating capacity (EDC) and electron-accepting capacity (EAC) of CDs were examined according to previously reported methods (Aeschbacher et al., 2010; Tan et al., 2017). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at open-circuit voltage and the impedance data covered the frequency range of 0.1–10,000 Hz. The amplitude of the sinusoidal AC voltage signal was 5 mV. The resulting impedance spectra were analyzed using ZSim Demo 3.3 software (Lyu et al., 2019).

The concentrations of SMX and its degradation products were determined using a high-performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC, Shimadzu LC-20AT, Japan) with a diode array detector. The detection wavelength was 270 nm. A Sapphire C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm) was used. The flow rate, injection volume and column temperature were 1.0 mL/min, 10 μL and 35 °C, respectively. The compositions of the mobile phases of detecting SMX, 4-ABSA and 4-aminothiophenol were as follows: 40% acetonitrile/60% ultrapure water containing 0.5% acetic acid, 12.5% methanol/87.5% ultrapure water containing 0.1% tetraethylammonium bromide, and 15% methanol/85% ultrapure water containing 1% methanoic acid, respectively.

The degradation products of SMX were detected by using a HPLC–mass spectrometer (Triple-TOF LC-MS 5600, AB SCIEX, USA) supplemented with a C18 column (Eclipse Plus C18, 2.1 × 100 mm, 1.7 μm). The mobile phase consisted of 40% acetonitrile and 60% ultrapure water containing 0.5% acetic acid at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. Electrospray ionization was accomplished in the positive mode with a capillary voltage of 3.5 kV. The gas flow was set at 6.0 L/min (550 °C), and the nebulizer was set at 50 psi.

Fe(II) and Fe(III) concentrations were determined by 1,10-phenanthroline method (Harvey et al., 1955). ATP content of cells was measured by microbial cell activity assay kit (BacTiter GloTM, Germany). ETSA was tested using the reported method by Liu et al. (2022). NADH/NAD+ and NADH of cells in the reaction systems were measured using NAD+/NADH assay kit with WST-8 (Beyotime, China). The detection of cellular complex I and complex III in the reaction system was referred to Zhang et al. (2021). Briefly, complex I activity was determined by monitoring the consumption of NADH (50 μM) at 340 nm; complex III activity was determined by monitoring the reduction of cytochrome c (50 μM) at 550 nm. The data were presented as the mean ± SD with three replicate samples. Statistical analysis was conducted using a two-tailed Student's t-test (GraphPad Prism 8.0.2). Statistically significant differences were considered when p-values were less than 0.05 (ns: p > 0.05, *: p < 0.05, **: p < 0.01, and ***: p ≤ 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size effects of nano-BCs on SMX degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The electron transfer capacities of nano-BCs with sizes of <100 nm were tested and compared with that of SHA. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the EDCs and EACs of nano-BCs were obviously higher than those of SHA. Moreover, the EDCs of nano-BCs were higher compared with their EACs, indicating that their electron-donating abilities were better and the redox-active groups were mainly reducing groups. Especially, CDs (<10 nm, 0.34 mmol e−/g) exhibited better electron-donating abilities than nano-BCs (10–100 nm, 0.23 mmol e−/g). The sum of EDC and EAC of CDs was also higher, indicating better electron transfer abilities of CDs. Moreover, EIS analysis showed that CDs are of better conductivity than nano-BCs with sizes of 10–100 nm (Fig. S2).

Based on the above results, the effects of the two nano-BCs on the degradation of 15 mg/L SMX by S. oneidensis MR-1 were compared (Fig. 1b). The results showed that SMX degradation process followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation (Table S1). In the presence of 100 mg/L CDs, the pseudo-first-order rate constant of SMX degradation (0.010 h−1) was higher compared with that (0.007 h−1) in the presence of 100 mg/L nano-BCs (10–100 nm). When Fe(III) was added to the above reaction systems, SMX degradation rate was further increased. Especially, the CDs-mediated degradation rate of SMX (0.013 h−1) was higher than the latter (0.010 h−1). EIS analysis showed that the introduction of Fe(III) could increase the conductivities of the two nano-BCs (Fig. S2). Moreover, the conductivity of the coexistence of CDs and Fe(III) was higher compared with that of the nano-BCs (10–100 nm) and Fe(III), indicating that CDs might interact with Fe(III) for electron transfer. After 36 h of the reaction, the effects of two types of nano-BCs on the ETSA of strain MR-1 were also determined (Fig. 1c). The results showed that the ETSA of strain MR-1 increased by 16.2% and 32.2% in the presence of nano-BCs (10–100 nm) and CDs (<10 nm), respectively. Therefore, CDs were selected for subsequent experiments.

The morphology and size distribution of CDs were characterized using TEM. Fig. 2 a shows that CDs were sphere-like with an average particle size of 7.45 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis showed that CDs exhibited clear lattice stripes with a distance of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2b) (Wang et al., 2020). The crystalline structure of CDs was further investigated by XRD (Fig. 2c), and the broad peak at 22.5° corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. Further analysis (Fig. 3 a) showed that the characteristic peaks of O–H/N–H at 3420/3280 cm−1 appeared in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, indicating that CDs are of certain hydrophilicity. The stretching vibration peaks of C=C/C=N at 1403 cm−1 (Zhang et al., 2023) were observed. Thus, the sp2 graphitic carbon structure of CDs was further confirmed, endowing CDs with the conductivity. Meanwhile, Aromatic Ar–H, Ar–O and C=O bands were found at 3070 cm−1, 1315 cm−1 and 1667 cm−1, respectively. These oxygen-containing groups could be used as redox sites for electron transfer. The above results were also demonstrated by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3, the peaks at 284.8 eV for C=C/C–C, 286.2 eV for C–O/O–C=O (Ntzoufra et al., 2021) and 288.1 eV for C=O appeared in the C 1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3b). Moreover, peaks at 531.3 eV for C=O were also observed in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c). According to the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), there were three N species of graphitic-N (401.00 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.67 eV) and pyidinic-N (398.58 eV). Graphitic-N was reported to perform good electrocatalytic activity and facilitate the electron transfer (Zhang et al., 2019).

The effect of Fe(III) concentration on SMX degradation by the Fe(III)-reducing strain MR-1 was tested using Fe(III) as an electron acceptor. Fig. S3 shows that the degradation rate of 10 mg/L SMX in the presence of 2 mM Fe(III) reached the highest (0.013 h−1) among the tested Fe(III) range of 1–6 mM. When Fe(III) concentration was over 2 mM, SMX degradation rate decreased with the increase of Fe(III) concentration. High concentration of Fe(III) (over 2 mM) easily formed precipitate, which was not beneficial for electron transfer from cells to solid Fe(III). In the presence of 2 mM Fe(III), the effect of CDs concentration on the degradation of 10 mg/L SMX was further tested. Fig. 4 a shows that 100 mg/L CDs was the optimal concentration for SMX degradation, during which the degradation rate of SMX was significantly increased by 84.6% compared to the control group. After 36 h of the reaction, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release assays were performed to assess the damage of cell membranes. A lower LDH release indicates a higher membrane integrity. Fig. 4b shows that, when the concentration of CDs was increased from 50 to 200 mg/L, the LDH release gradually increased. Especially, compared to the control group, there were 17.7% and 25.9% increase of LDH release in the presence of 150 and 200 mg/L CDs, respectively. This result indicated that the high concentration of CDs (over 100 mg/L) caused the obvious cytotoxicity, resulting in the decrease of SMX degradation rate (Fig. 4a). In the presence of 100 mg/L CDs and 2 mM Fe(III), the degradation rate constant of 10 mg/L SMX was only 0.001 h−1 in the absence of cells, indicating that SMX removal is mainly a biodegradation process. In the presence of cells, Fe(III) (0.013 h−1) and CDs (0.012 h−1) had a similar contribution to promote SMX degradation (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, the coexistence of Fe(III) and CDs resulted in a significant increase in SMX degradation rate (0.024 h−1).

The effect of SMX concentration on its degradation was also investigated (Fig. 5b). The results showed that the degradation rate of SMX decreased with the increase of its concentration. Among the tested SMX concentrations ranging from 10 mg/L-30 mg/L, the addition of 100 mg/L CDs could increase the degradation rate of SMX. Especially, when the initial concentration of SMX was 10 mg/L, the rate constant of SMX degradation (0.024 h−1) in CDs-amended system reached the highest, which was over 1.8-fold higher compared with the control (0.013 h−1) without CDs. Under this condition, the degradation efficiency of SMX reached 80.1% in 72 h. During this process, the degradation products of SMX were analyzed using LC-MS. As shown in Fig. S4, three peaks with m/z 99, m/z 173 and m/z 126 appeared, which were proposed to be 3-amino-5-methylisoxazole (3A5MI), 4-ABSA and 4-aminothiophenol, respectively. According to the three metabolites, SMX degradation pathway was proposed (Fig. S5). Firstly, SMX was hydrolyzed to 3A5MI and 4-ABSA via the cleavage of the S–N bond, and then the latter was further reduced to 4-aminothiophene, which was consistent with previous reports (Mao et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018). This result showed that CDs addition did not change the degradation pathway of SMX. CDs were only used as electron mediators for accelerating SMX degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of CDs during SMX degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Further studies showed that Fe(III) reduction efficiency in the CDs-amended system reached 66.2%, which was higher than that without CDs (41.0%) in 72 h, implying that CDs addition accelerated Fe(III) reduction (Fig. 6 ). Moreover, Fe(III) addition could increase the conductivity of CDs and decrease their FL intensity (Figs. S2 and S7), which meant that some CDs could be combined with Fe(III). Further XPS analysis of the used CDs demonstrated that Fe was introduced and the four peaks in the Fe 2p spectrum were observed at 710.0 eV, 714.8 eV, 723.1 eV and 728.1 eV, which belong to Fe (II) 2p3/2, Fe (III) 2p3/2, Fe (II) 2p1/2 and Fe (II) 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. S8a). In addition, compared to the initial CDs, the N 1s peaks of the used CDs shifted to higher binding energies due to decreased electron density (Fig. S8b), which could be attributed to the binding of CDs to Fe(III) (Li et al., 2022). Previous studies showed that outer-membrane c-type cytochromes MtrC and OmcA of strain MR-1 could be responsible for the reduction of extracellular Fe(III) (Shi et al., 2006). Bulk BCs as an electron shuttle could facilitate electron transfer between microorganisms and Fe(III) (Zhou et al., 2016). As CDs were of EDC and EAC, the electrons from cells could be transferred to extracellular Fe(III) via CDs, resulting in the presence of Fe (II) in the used CDs (Fig. S8a).

During SMX degradation, it is worth noting that the bio-reduction process from 4-ABSA to 4-aminothiophenol may be also affected by extracellular Fe(III) reduction and CDs. Our studies found that Fe(III) and CDs could promote the reduction of 4-ABSA (Fig. S6), respectively. This result indicated that cells could transfer the electrons to 4-ABSA via Fe(III) or CDs. However, the coexistence of Fe(III) and CDs resulted in a decreased reduction rate of 4-ABSA compared with those without Fe(III) or CDs. As CDs could bind with c-type cytochromes MtrC and OmcA of strain MR-1 responsible for Fe(III) reduction (Yang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2013), there is an electron competition between extracellular Fe(III) reduction and CDs during the 4-ABSA reduction process. Even so, in the presence of both Fe(III) and CDs, the reduction rate constant of 4-ABSA was 1.46-fold higher than that without Fe(III) and CDs. The above results indicated that extracellular CDs participated in extracellular electron transfer for SMX degradation and Fe(III) reduction. The analysis of the used CDs showed that functional groups of C=O and graphite-N could be also found in the XPS (Figs. S8b–d), indicating that the electrical conductivity and redox properties of CDs are still retained.

For the secreted flavins from strain MR-1, our studies showed that the concentration of extracellular flavins (98.9 nM) in the CDs-amended system was over 2.0-fold higher compared with those (49.9 nM) without CDs in 72 h. It was reported that the secreted flavins had little effect on the reduction of soluble Fe(III) (Kotloski et al., 2013). Moreover, when around 90 nM riboflavin was further added into the CDs-amended system, there was little increase in the SMX degradation rate. Thus, the secreted flavins had little effect on SMX degradation due to their low concentration.

CDs distribution in the reaction system was further investigated. Considering that 2 g/L yeast extract had a certain FL, 4 g/L lactate was used in this experiment to replace the mixture of 2 g/L lactate and 2 g/L yeast extract. The result showed that extracellular FL intensity gradually decreased with the increase of reaction time, implying that partial CDs entered into the cells (Fig. 7 a). For these cells, the FL peaks observed in the periplasm and cytoplasm mixtures were similar with that of CDs (Fig. 7b). This finding indicated that intracellular CDs were mainly located in the periplasm and cytoplasm. Cell extracts experiments showed that the SMX-degrading activities of cell membrane, and the mixture of periplasm and cytoplasm from strain MR-1 were 37.8 and 72.8 U/mg protein, respectively. In the presence of CDs, their activities were increased up to 69.9 U/mg proteins and 158.6 U/mg proteins, respectively. These results showed that intracellular CDs facilitated electron transfer for the initial degradation of SMX. Based on the involvement of extracellular CDs in the reduction of 4-ABSA as an intermediate metabolite of SMX, both extracellular and intracellular processes were involved in SMX degradation.

During SMX degradation, the effect of CDs on the metabolic activity of cells was also tested. Fig. 8 a shows that ATP content of cells was increased by 25.2% in CDs-amended system, implying that CDs enhanced the activity of strain MR-1. The level of NADH, a direct electron donor, can be used to estimate the electron-producing capacity of an organism. A high intracellular NADH/NAD+ ratio is an advantage for the reduction process and crucial for controlling microbial redox homeostasis (Ebert et al., 2011). As depicted in Fig. 8b, the increases of NADH and NADH/NAD+ ratio of cells were observed in CDs-amended system compared with those without CDs. Moreover, the activities of complex I and complex III in the CDs-amended system were 1.77- and 1.22-fold higher than those without CDs, respectively (Fig. 8c). This finding suggested that CDs accelerated intracellular electron transfer.

Previous studies showed that additional AQDS and riboflavin coupled with Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle could enhance SMX degradation via increasing extracellular electron transfer (Zhou et al., 2018). This performance was attributed to their redox properties. Biochar was ever demonstrated to remove SMX via the adsorption effect (Yao et al., 2012). In the present study, SHA-derived nano-BCs had little adsorption effect on SMX removal due to their good solubility. Compared with nano-BCs (10–100 nm), CDs exhibited better accelerating performance on SMX degradation via two pathways. Firstly, extracellular CDs promoted Fe(III) reduction and participated in the degradation of the intermediate metabolite 4-ABSA. Secondly, CDs directly accelerated intracellular electron transfer via entering into cells. Moreover, CDs enhanced the activity of SMX-degrading enzymes located in the cells. These results showed that CDs participated in extracellular and intracellular electron transfer processes for SMX degradation. Further studies will be carried out on the detailed contribution of the extracellular and intracellular processes to SMX degradation. As CDs can be absorbed by cells, pre-incubation of CDs and cells is suggested for CDs application during SMX removal.

SHA-derived CDs exhibited good electron transfer ability. CDs could be used as electron mediators to accelerate SMX degradation using Fe(III) as an electron acceptor. In the presence of 100 mg/L CDs, the degradation rate of 10 mg/L SMX was increased by 84.6% compared with that without CDs. During this process, extracellular and intracellular CDs participated in SMX degradation. Meanwhile, CDs enhanced the activities and electron transfer abilities of cells by internalizing them into the cells. These results provide a new insight into the promoting effect of CDs on SMX degradation and an approach for SHA utilization.

Peiwen Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qiansheng Li: Writing – original draft. Hong Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ze Fu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiti Zhou: Supervision, Resources. Chenghao Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xuehai Wang: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Open Foundation of National Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Petrochemical Environmental Pollution Prevention and Control Technology (Grant No. 34880000-22-ZC0607-0117).
